content:
# Genesis Chapter 34 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 19v–20r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_034_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_034_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 31–32 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0035 | https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0036

---

*Dinah Goes Out — Shechem Takes Her (vv. 1–4)*

**34:1** And Dinah [וַתֵּצֵא דִינָה] — the daughter of Leah [בַּת-לֵאָה] whom she had borne to Jacob [אֲשֶׁר יָלְדָה לְיַעֲקֹב] — went out [וַתֵּצֵא] to see [לִרְאוֹת] the daughters of [בִּבְנוֹת] the land [הָאָרֶץ].

**34:2** And Shechem [וַיַּרְא אֹתָהּ שְׁכֶם] the son of Hamor [בֶּן-חֲמוֹר] the Hivite [הַחִוִּי] — the prince of [נְשִׂיא] the land [הָאָרֶץ] — saw her [וַיַּרְא אֹתָהּ] — and he took her [וַיִּקַּח אֹתָהּ] and he lay with her [וַיִּשְׁכַּב אֹתָהּ] and he humiliated her [וַיְעַנֶּהָ].

**34:3** And his soul clung [וַתִּדְבַּק נַפְשׁוֹ] to Dinah [בְּדִינָה] the daughter of Jacob [בַּת-יַעֲקֹב] — and he loved [וַיֶּאֱהַב] the young woman [אֶת-הַנַּעֲרָ] — and he spoke [וַיְדַבֵּר] to the heart of [עַל-לֵב] the young woman [הַנַּעֲרָ].

**34:4** And Shechem said [וַיֹּאמֶר שְׁכֶם] to Hamor [אֶל-חֲמוֹר] his father [אָבִיו] — saying [לֵאמֹר] — Take for me [קַח-לִי] this young woman [אֶת-הַיַּלְדָּה הַזֹּאת] as a wife [לְאִשָּׁה].

---

*Jacob Hears — Hamor Goes to Jacob (vv. 5–7)*

**34:5** And Jacob heard [וְיַעֲקֹב שָׁמַע] that he had defiled [כִּי טִמֵּא] Dinah [אֶת-דִּינָה] his daughter [בִּתּוֹ] — but his sons [וּבָנָיו] were with his livestock [הָיוּ אֶת-מִקְנֵהוּ] in the field [בַּשָּׂדֶה] — and Jacob held his peace [וְהֶחֱרִשׁ יַעֲקֹב] until they came [עַד-בֹּאָם].

**34:6** And Hamor [וַיֵּצֵא חֲמוֹר] — the father of Shechem [אֲבִי-שְׁכֶם] — went out [וַיֵּצֵא] to Jacob [אֶל-יַעֲקֹב] to speak with him [לְדַבֵּר אִתּוֹ].

**34:7** And the sons of Jacob [וּבְנֵי יַעֲקֹב] came from the field [בָּאוּ מִן-הַשָּׂדֶה] when they heard [כְּשָׁמְעָם] — and the men [וַיִּתְעַצְּבוּ הָאֲנָשִׁים] were pained [וַיִּתְעַצְּבוּ] — and it burned very greatly to them [וַיִּחַר לָהֶם מְאֹד] — for a disgrace [כִּי נְבָלָה] had been done in Israel [עָשָׂה בְיִשְׂרָאֵל] — to lie with [לִשְׁכַּב אֶת] the daughter of Jacob [בַּת-יַעֲקֹב] — and such a thing [וְכֵן] is not to be done [לֹא יֵעָשֶׂה].

---

*Hamor's Proposal of Intermarriage (vv. 8–12)*

**34:8** And Hamor spoke [וַיְדַבֵּר חֲמוֹר] with them [אִתָּם] — saying [לֵאמֹר] — Shechem [שְׁכֶם] — my son [בְּנִי] — his soul longs [חָשְׁקָה נַפְשׁוֹ] for your daughter [בְּבִתְּכֶם] — give her [תְּנוּ נָא אֹתָהּ] — please — to him as a wife [לוֹ לְאִשָּׁה].

**34:9** And make yourselves related by marriage [וְהִתְחַתְּנוּ] with us [אֹתָנוּ] — your daughters [בְּנֹתֵיכֶם] you shall give [תִּתְּנוּ-לָנוּ] to us — and our daughters [וְאֶת-בְּנֹתֵינוּ] you shall take [תִּקְחוּ לָכֶם] for yourselves.

**34:10** And with us [וְאִתָּנוּ] you shall dwell [תֵּשֵׁבוּ] — and the land [וְהָאָרֶץ] shall be before you [תִּהְיֶה לִפְנֵיכֶם] — dwell [שְׁבוּ] and trade in it [וּסְחָרוּהָ] and take holdings in it [וְהֵאָחֲזוּ בָהּ].

**34:11** And Shechem said [וַיֹּאמֶר שְׁכֶם] to her father [אֶל-אָבִיהָ] and to her brothers [וְאֶל-אַחֶיהָ] — let me find [אֶמְצָא-חֵן] favor [חֵן] in your eyes [בְּעֵינֵיכֶם] — and whatever [וַאֲשֶׁר] you say [תֹּאמְרוּ] to me [אֵלַי] I will give [אֶתֵּן].

**34:12** Make the bride-price [הַרְבּוּ עָלַי מְאֹד מֹהַר] very great upon me [הַרְבּוּ עָלַי] and the gift [וּמַתָּן] — and I will give [וְאֶתְּנָה] as you say [כַּאֲשֶׁר תֹּאמְרוּ] to me [אֵלַי] — and give to me [וּתְנוּ-לִי] the young woman [אֶת-הַנַּעֲרָ] as a wife [לְאִשָּׁה].

---

*The Sons of Jacob Answer with Deceit — the Circumcision Demand (vv. 13–17)*

**34:13** And the sons of Jacob [וַיַּעֲנוּ בְנֵי-יַעֲקֹב] answered [וַיַּעֲנוּ] Shechem [אֶת-שְׁכֶם] and Hamor [וְאֶת-חֲמוֹר] his father [אָבִיו] with deceit [בְּמִרְמָה] — and they spoke [וַיְדַבְּרוּ] — because [אֲשֶׁר] he had defiled [טִמֵּא] Dinah [אֵת דִּינָה] their sister [אֲחֹתָם].

**34:14** And they said [וַיֹּאמְרוּ] to them [אֲלֵיהֶם] — We are not able [לֹא נוּכַל] to do [לַעֲשׂוֹת] this thing [הַדָּבָר הַזֶּה] — to give [לָתֵת] our sister [אֶת-אֲחֹתֵנוּ] to a man [לְאִישׁ] who [אֲשֶׁר] has a foreskin [עָרְלָה לוֹ] — for [כִּי] that [הוּא] is a reproach [חֶרְפָּה] to us [לָנוּ].

**34:15** Only [אַךְ] in this [בְּזֹאת] will we consent [נֵאוֹת לָכֶם] to you — if [אִם] you become [תִּהְיוּ] as we are [כָּמֹנוּ] — for every male among you [לְהִמֹּל לָכֶם כָּל-זָכָר] to be circumcised [לְהִמֹּל].

**34:16** Then we will give [וְנָתַנּוּ] our daughters [אֶת-בְּנֹתֵינוּ] to you [לָכֶם] — and your daughters [וְאֶת-בְּנֹתֵיכֶם] we will take [נִקַּח-לָנוּ] for ourselves — and we will dwell [וְיָשַׁבְנוּ] with you [אִתְּכֶם] and we will become [וְהָיִינוּ] one people [לְעַם אֶחָד].

**34:17** But if [וְאִם] you will not listen [לֹא תִשְׁמְעוּ] to us [אֵלֵינוּ] to be circumcised [לְהִמּוֹל] — then we will take [וְלָקַחְנוּ] our daughter [אֶת-בִּתֵּנוּ] and we will go [וְהָלָכְנוּ].

---

*Hamor and Shechem Persuade Their City (vv. 18–24)*

**34:18** And their words [וַיִּיטְבוּ דִבְרֵיהֶם] were good [וַיִּיטְבוּ] in the eyes of [בְּעֵינֵי] Hamor [חֲמוֹר] and in the eyes of [וּבְעֵינֵי] Shechem [שְׁכֶם] the son of Hamor [בֶּן-חֲמוֹר].

**34:19** And the young man [וְלֹא-אֵחַר הַנַּעַר] did not delay [לֹא-אֵחַר] to do [לַעֲשׂוֹת] the thing [הַדָּבָר] — for he delighted [כִּי חָפֵץ] in the daughter of Jacob [בְּבַת-יַעֲקֹב] — and he [וְהוּא] was the most honored [נִכְבָּד] of all [מִכֹּל] the house of his father [בֵּית אָבִיו].

**34:20** And Hamor [וַיָּבֹא חֲמוֹר] and Shechem [וּשְׁכֶם בְּנוֹ] his son [בְּנוֹ] came [וַיָּבֹא] to the gate [אֶל-שַׁעַר] of their city [עִירָם] — and they spoke [וַיְדַבְּרוּ] to the men of their city [אֶל-אַנְשֵׁי עִירָם] — saying [לֵאמֹר].

**34:21** These men [הָאֲנָשִׁים הָאֵלֶּה] are at peace [שְׁלֵמִים הֵם] with us [אִתָּנוּ] — and let them dwell [וְיֵשְׁבוּ] in the land [בָּאָרֶץ] and trade in it [וְיִסְחֲרוּ אֹתָהּ] — and the land [וְהָאָרֶץ] behold [הִנֵּה] it is wide of hands [רַחֲבַת יָדַיִם] before them [לִפְנֵיהֶם] — their daughters [אֶת-בְּנֹתָם] we will take [נִקַּח-לָנוּ] as wives [לְנָשִׁים] and our daughters [וְאֶת-בְּנֹתֵינוּ] we will give [נִתֵּן] to them.

**34:22** Only [אַךְ] in this [בְּזֹאת] will the men consent [יֵאֹתוּ לָנוּ הָאֲנָשִׁים] to us — to dwell [לָשֶׁבֶת] with us [אִתָּנוּ] — to be [לִהְיוֹת] one people [לְעַם אֶחָד] — in every male among us [בְּהִמּוֹל לָנוּ כָּל-זָכָר] being circumcised [לְהִמּוֹל] as they [כַּאֲשֶׁר הֵם] are circumcised [נִמֹּלִים].

**34:23** Their livestock [מִקְנֵהֶם] and their acquisition [וְקִנְיָנָם] and all their cattle [וְכָל-בְּהֶמְתָּם] — shall they not [הֲלוֹא] be ours [לָנוּ הֵם] — only [אַךְ] let us consent [נֵאוֹתָה] to them [לָהֶם] and they will dwell [וְיֵשְׁבוּ] with us [אִתָּנוּ].

**34:24** And they listened [וַיִּשְׁמְעוּ] to Hamor [אֶל-חֲמוֹר] and to Shechem [וְאֶל-שְׁכֶם] his son [בְּנוֹ] — all [כָּל] who went out of [יֹצְאֵי] the gate of his city [שַׁעַר עִירוֹ] — and every male [וַיִּמֹּלוּ כָּל-זָכָר] was circumcised [וַיִּמֹּלוּ] — all who went out of [כָּל-יֹצְאֵי] the gate of his city [שַׁעַר עִירוֹ].

---

*Simeon and Levi's Massacre (vv. 25–29)*

**34:25** And it came to pass [וַיְהִי] on the third day [בַּיּוֹם הַשְּׁלִישִׁי] — when they were in pain [בִּהְיוֹתָם כֹּאֲבִים] — that two of the sons of Jacob [וַיִּקְחוּ שְׁנֵי-בְנֵי-יַעֲקֹב] — Simeon [שִׁמְעוֹן] and Levi [וְלֵוִי] — brothers of Dinah [אֲחֵי דִינָה] — each took [וַיִּקְחוּ] his sword [אִישׁ חַרְבּוֹ] and they came [וַיָּבֹאוּ] upon the city [עַל-הָעִיר] in security [בֶּטַח] — and they killed [וַיַּהַרְגוּ] every male [כָּל-זָכָר].

**34:26** And Hamor [וְאֶת-חֲמוֹר] and Shechem [וְאֶת-שְׁכֶם] his son [בְּנוֹ] they killed [הָרָגוּ] by the mouth of the sword [לְפִי-חָרֶב] — and they took [וַיִּקְחוּ] Dinah [אֶת-דִּינָה] from the house of Shechem [מִבֵּית שְׁכֶם] and they went out [וַיֵּצֵאוּ].

**34:27** The sons of Jacob [בְּנֵי יַעֲקֹב] came [בָּאוּ] upon the slain [עַל-הַחֲלָלִים] and they plundered [וַיָּבֹזּוּ] the city [אֶת-הָעִיר] — because [אֲשֶׁר] they had defiled [טִמְּאוּ] their sister [אֲחֹתָם].

**34:28** Their flocks [אֶת-צֹאנָם] and their cattle [וְאֶת-בְּקָרָם] and their donkeys [וְאֶת-חֲמֹרֵיהֶם] — and whatever [וְאֵת] was in the city [אֲשֶׁר-בָּעִיר] and whatever [וְאֶת-אֲשֶׁר] was in the field [בַּשָּׂדֶה] — they took [לָקָחוּ].

**34:29** And all their wealth [וְאֶת-כָּל-חֵילָם] and all their little-ones [וְאֶת-כָּל-טַפָּם] and their wives [וְאֶת-נְשֵׁיהֶם] they captured [שָׁבוּ] — and they plundered [וַיָּבֹזּוּ] — even all [וְאֵת כָּל] that was in the house [אֲשֶׁר בַּבַּיִת].

---

*Jacob Rebukes Simeon and Levi (v. 30)*

**34:30** And Jacob said [וַיֹּאמֶר יַעֲקֹב] to Simeon [אֶל-שִׁמְעוֹן] and to Levi [וְאֶל-לֵוִי] — You have brought trouble upon me [עֲכַרְתֶּם אֹתִי] — to make me stink [לְהַבְאִישֵׁנִי] among the inhabitants [בְּישֵׁב] of the land [הָאָרֶץ] — among the Canaanites [בַּכְּנַעֲנִי] and among the Perizzites [וּבַפְּרִזִּי] — and I am few in number [וַאֲנִי מְתֵי מִסְפָּר] — and they will gather against me [וְנֶאֶסְפוּ עָלַי] and they will strike me [וְהִכּוּנִי] — and I will be destroyed [וְנִשְׁמַדְתִּי] — I and my house [אֲנִי וּבֵיתִי].

---

*The Sons' Defiant Reply (v. 31)*

**34:31** And they said [וַיֹּאמְרוּ] — Shall he treat our sister [הַכְזוֹנָה יַעֲשֶׂה] as a prostitute [הַכְזוֹנָה]?

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

**On reading the Leningrad Codex, Folios 19v–20r:**

LC page 1 (GEN_034_page_001.jpg) is folio 19v of the Leningrad Codex, photographed in color by the West Semitic Research Project. The three-column Sephardic square script carries the parasha opening for Genesis 34 — visible at the bottom portion of the previous folio (19v carries the end of Genesis 33, vv. 16–20, and then opens Genesis 34). The chapter begins with the section break after the Genesis 33 conclusion: the altar inscription אֵל אֱלֹהֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל concludes chapter 33, and then the new pericope begins with וַתֵּצֵא דִינָה. The three columns are clearly legible with full Tiberian vocalization and cantillation marks. The verso of this folio (19v) carries Genesis 34 from verse 1 through approximately verse 20, with the circumcision demand and the city-gate persuasion scene. The script is consistent and clear throughout; a few cantillation marks at the page edge are slightly clipped but the text is complete.

LC page 2 (GEN_034_page_002.jpg) is folio 20r. It carries Genesis 34 from approximately verse 21 through verse 31 (the complete conclusion), followed by the opening verses of Genesis 35. The massacre narrative (vv. 25–29), Jacob's rebuke (v. 30), and the sons' devastating final question (v. 31) are all clearly legible on this folio. The folio number כ (twenty) is visible in the upper corner. The verse כִּי-חָשְׁקָה נַפְשׁוֹ בְּבַת-יַעֲקֹב ("for his soul had longed for the daughter of Jacob," v. 19) is clearly readable. The final verse — הַכְזוֹנָה יַעֲשֶׂה אֶת-אֲחֹתֵנוּ ("shall he treat our sister as a prostitute?") — ends the chapter as a terse, one-clause defiant question with no narrative resolution.

**On reading Codex Vaticanus, Pages 31–32:**

Page 31 (GEN_034_vaticanus_page_001.jpg) bears the chapter annotation CAP. XXXVIII in the right margin, which is the LXX chapter number corresponding to the MT's Genesis 34 material. The continuous Greek uncial script, without word-breaks, carries the opening of Genesis 34 from verse 1. The three-column layout is clearly visible. This page carries verses 1 through approximately verse 17 of the chapter.

Page 32 (GEN_034_vaticanus_page_002.jpg) has the chapter header Γεν. XXXIX at the top center, indicating the next chapter begins on this page. The page carries Genesis 34 from approximately verse 18 through verse 31, followed by the opening of Genesis 35. The text is legible in continuous uncial script throughout.

---

**34:2 — Three Escalating Verbs of Violence (*vayyiqqach / vayyishkav / vay'anneha*, וַיִּקַּח / וַיִּשְׁכַּב / וַיְעַנֶּהָ):**

This verse contains the three-verb sequence at the center of the chapter's moral weight, and EMET renders each with full literalism:

1. **וַיִּקַּח אֹתָהּ** (*vayyiqqach otah*, "and he took her") — the verb לָקַח (*laqach*, "to take, to seize, to appropriate") with the direct object pronoun *otah* ("her"). The verb is the same used throughout Genesis for legitimate taking of a wife (e.g., 4:19, 11:29, 24:3). Here, before any consent or negotiation, the verb of acquisition strips Dinah of all agency: she is taken as an object.

2. **וַיִּשְׁכַּב אֹתָהּ** (*vayyishkav otah*, "and he lay with her") — the verb שָׁכַב (*shakhav*, "to lie down") used here in its sexual sense. The construction שָׁכַב אֶת (*shakhav et*) rather than שָׁכַב עִם (*shakhav 'im*, "to lie with") is significant: *et* is the direct-object marker, not the preposition "with." The syntax treats Dinah as the object of the act, not a participant. This syntactic distinction is not recoverable in standard English ("lay with her" reads the same either way) but EMET notes it. Several scholars argue the *et* construction rather than *'im* signals rape rather than consensual intercourse throughout biblical Hebrew narrative.

3. **וַיְעַנֶּהָ** (*vay'anneha*, "and he humiliated her / violated her / afflicted her") — the Piel of עָנָה (*'anah*, "to humble, to afflict, to force, to violate"). The Piel intensively active stem with the suffix "her": he actively, forcefully humiliated/violated her. This verb appears in the same form in Judges 19:24–25 (the gang rape at Gibeah), Judges 20:5, and 2 Samuel 13:12, 14, 22, 32 (the rape of Tamar by Amnon — where the same three-verb cluster appears). The use of *'innah* (Piel of *'anah*) is the biblical narrator's own moral verdict: whatever verb cluster precedes it, *'innah* marks the act as violation. There is no ambiguity in the Hebrew: the third verb establishes that what happened to Dinah was a crime.

The LXX Vaticanus reads: *kai idōn autēn Sychem... elaben autēn kai ekoimēthē met' autēs kai etapeinōsen autēn* (καὶ ἰδὼν αὐτὴν Συχέμ... ἔλαβεν αὐτὴν καὶ ἐκοιμήθη μετ' αὐτῆς καὶ ἐταπείνωσεν αὐτήν) — "Shechem saw her... took her and slept with her and humiliated/demeaned her." The LXX uses *etapeinōsen* (ἐταπείνωσεν, "he humiliated/debased") for the MT's *vay'anneha*, corresponding exactly. The LXX does use *met' autēs* ("with her") rather than the direct-object construction of the MT, softening the syntactic indicator of rape slightly.

---

**34:3 — Love Language After Violence: *vattidbaq nafsho* and *vayedabber 'al-lev* (וַתִּדְבַּק נַפְשׁוֹ / וַיְדַבֵּר עַל-לֵב):**

The juxtaposition of verse 2 (violent violation) and verse 3 (tender language of attachment and wooing) is one of the most unsettling literary structures in Genesis. The Hebrew narrator does not resolve the contradiction — he holds it.

**וַתִּדְבַּק נַפְשׁוֹ בְּדִינָה** (*vattidbaq nafsho be-Dinah*) — "And his soul clung to Dinah." The verb דָּבַק (*davaq*, "to cling, to cleave, to hold fast") is the same verb used in Genesis 2:24 for the marriage bond: *ve-davaq be-ishto* ("and he shall cling to his wife"). The noun נֶפֶשׁ (*nefesh*, "soul, self, inner life, throat, appetite") indicates the seat of desire and personhood. The phrase says that Shechem's innermost self attached to Dinah — the language is of genuine, deep attachment. Coming immediately after the act of violation, it is deeply disorienting.

**וַיְדַבֵּר עַל-לֵב הַנַּעֲרָ** (*vayedabber 'al-lev hanna'arah*, "and he spoke to the heart of the young woman") — the idiom דִּבֶּר עַל-לֵב (*dibber 'al-lev*, "to speak upon/to the heart") is biblical Hebrew for tenderness, comfort, and wooing. It appears in Ruth 2:13 (Boaz to Ruth), in 2 Samuel 19:8 (speaking comfort to the troops), in Hosea 2:14 (God wooing Israel), and paradigmatically in Genesis 50:21 (Joseph comforting his brothers). The phrase is the language of a lover's persuasion and consolation — words spoken not merely *to* someone but *into* their heart.

That a rapist is described in the language of deep love and tender speech is not a narrative endorsement of his act. It is the narrator's unflinching record of the complexity of the situation — the same complexity that later makes the chapter's resolution so brutal and unresolved. EMET translates both phrases literally and declines to harmonize them.

The LXX Vaticanus reads *kai proseschē tēi psychēi Dinan... kai ēgapēsen tēn parthenon kai elalēsen kata tēn dianoian tēs parthenou* — "and [his soul] was attached to Dinah... and he loved the maiden and spoke according to the mind of the maiden." The LXX uses *parthenos* (παρθένος, "maiden/virgin") where the MT has *na'arah* (נַעֲרָ, "young woman") — a slight elevation of Dinah's status that underscores what was violated.

---

**34:7 — *nevalah be-Yisra'el* (נְבָלָה בְּיִשְׂרָאֵל) — "A Disgrace in Israel": First Occurrence of a Judicial Formula:**

The phrase נְבָלָה בְּיִשְׂרָאֵל (*nevalah be-Yisra'el*, "a disgrace/outrage/foolish thing in Israel") appears here for the first time in the Hebrew Bible, and its appearance is anachronistic in the most striking way: at the time of this narrative, "Israel" is a personal name just recently given to Jacob at Peniel (32:28). It is not yet a nation, not yet a people, not yet a covenant community with laws about sexual violations. And yet the narrator uses — and the sons invoke — a judicial formula that would become standard in later Israelite law: the *nevalah* language recurs in Deuteronomy 22:21 (of a bride found not to be a virgin: "she has committed a disgrace/foolishness in Israel"), Joshua 7:15 (Achan's sin: "he has committed a *nevalah* in Israel"), Judges 19:23, 20:6, 10 (the rape at Gibeah: "a *nevalah* and a *zimmah* in Israel"), and 2 Samuel 13:12 (Tamar to Amnon: "do not do this *nevalah*").

The word נְבָלָה (*nevalah*) derives from the root נָבַל (*naval*, "to be foolish, to be degraded, to act basely") — the same root as the adjective נָבָל (*naval*, "fool, vile person") and the name נָבָל (*Naval*, the husband of Abigail, whose very name means Fool — 1 Samuel 25:25). *Nevalah* is not merely "foolishness" in the English sense of silliness; it is "an act that violates the foundational order of a community" — a moral and social outrage that unmakes communal bonds. In the legal contexts it often refers specifically to sexual violations.

The declaration *vekhen lo ye'aseh* (וְכֵן לֹא יֵעָשֶׂה, "and such a thing is not to be done") is the standard refrain that follows the *nevalah* formula in other passages (cf. 2 Samuel 13:12). It is normative, quasi-legal language. By applying this formula to what happened to Dinah, the narrator (and the brothers) are asserting that Shechem has violated not merely Dinah but the entire covenant order — even though that order is still forming.

EMET translates *nevalah* as "disgrace" — close to the range of the Hebrew without over-specifying. The LXX reads *anomia* (ἀνομία, "lawlessness") — which is theologically loaded but captures the legal-violation sense of the term.

---

**34:13 — *bemirmah* (בְּמִרְמָה) — "With Deceit": The Narrator's Verdict on the Brothers:**

The word בְּמִרְמָה (*bemirmah*, "with deceit, with cunning, with treachery") is the narrator's own characterization of the brothers' response. It is placed before the brothers' speech, as a framing device: *and the sons of Jacob answered Shechem and Hamor... with deceit.* The narrator does not merely report the brothers' words; he tells the reader in advance that what follows is a deception.

The noun מִרְמָה (*mirmah*, "deceit, treachery, fraud") appears throughout the Hebrew Bible in negative contexts. Notably, it is cognate with the name עָקֹב (*'aqov*, "crooked/supplanting," the root of יַעֲקֹב, Jacob's own name). Jacob's sons perpetrate the kind of deception that defined their father's early career — here in full circle, here against foreigners rather than family, here with lethal consequences.

The narrator's use of *bemirmah* is morally significant: it does not excuse what was done to Dinah, but it refuses to valorize the brothers' response. The massacre will follow from a plan made "with deceit." The chapter's moral universe is not one of heroes and villains; it is one of violation answered by violation, with the narrator bearing witness to both without resolution.

EMET translates *bemirmah* as "with deceit" — the plain meaning. The LXX reads *meta dolou* (μετὰ δόλου, "with guile/treachery") — the standard Greek equivalent, *dolos* being a common word for cunning deception.

---

**34:14 — *'arel* (עָרְלָה) — "One Who Has a Foreskin": Circumcision as Social Boundary:**

The brothers' refusal uses the word עָרְלָה (*'orlah*, "foreskin, uncircumcised state") as a marker of social, covenantal, and — from the brothers' perspective — moral disgrace. *Le'ish asher 'orlah lo* ("to a man who has a foreskin") and the declaration *ki-cherpah hi lanu* ("for that is a reproach/shame to us") frames uncircumcision as social incompatibility. The word חֶרְפָּה (*cherpah*, "reproach, shame, taunt") is a strong word: it is used of Dinah's own situation (v. 14), of the *nevalah* frame, and throughout the Psalms and prophets for national disgrace.

This is one of the passages that reveals circumcision's function as a social boundary marker in Israel, not merely a religious rite. The brothers invoke it instrumentally — as a pretext — and the Shechemites accept it in good faith. The deceit is enabled by the real weight of the covenantal boundary: the demand is believable precisely because circumcision *was* a genuine requirement for participation in Israel's covenant community.

---

**34:25 — *bihyotam ko'avim* (בִּהְיוֹתָם כֹּאֲבִים) — "When They Were in Pain": The Exploitation of Circumcision Vulnerability:**

The participial construction בִּהְיוֹתָם כֹּאֲבִים (*bihyotam ko'avim*, "in their being in pain / while they were in pain") specifies the precise timing of the massacre: not merely on the third day (when the surgical wound is most acute and debilitating), but specifically *while they were in pain*. The word כֹּאֲבִים (*ko'avim*, "those who are in pain") is the Qal active participle plural of כָּאַב (*ka'av*, "to be in pain, to ache, to suffer"). The text is clinical and precise: the brothers knew exactly when their targets would be most physically incapacitated, and they chose that moment.

The medical detail is exact: on the third day following circumcision, the surgical site is at peak inflammation and soreness (cf. Genesis 17:23–27, where the mass circumcision of Abraham's household is described with similar attention to timing). The men of Shechem had agreed to the circumcision in good faith, motivated partly by economic anticipation (v. 23: "shall not their livestock and their acquisition be ours?"). They were physically laid low by the very act they had performed in good faith to join the covenant. Simeon and Levi exploited this vulnerability deliberately.

The LXX Vaticanus reads *en tēi hēmera tēi tritēi, hote ēsan en tōi ponōi* — "on the third day, when they were in the pain" — *ponos* (πόνος, "pain, distress, labor") corresponding to MT *ko'ev*. Exact rendering.

---

**34:30 — Jacob's Rebuke: Pragmatic Fear, Not Moral Outrage (*'akartem oti* / *lehav'isheni*, עֲכַרְתֶּם אֹתִי / לְהַבְאִישֵׁנִי):**

Jacob's response to the massacre is the most morally disturbing feature of the entire chapter — not because he condemns his sons, but because of *what he says* and what he conspicuously *does not say*.

**עֲכַרְתֶּם אֹתִי** (*'akartem oti*, "you have brought trouble upon me / you have stirred up trouble for me") — the Qal perfect of עָכַר (*'akhar*, "to trouble, to stir up, to bring disaster upon"). This is the same verb used of Achan in Joshua 7:25 (*me 'akhar'tanu*, "why have you brought trouble upon us?") and in Judges 11:35 (Jephthah to his daughter: *'akartem oti*, "you have brought trouble upon me"). It is the verb of a man who is troubled — not because a massacre has occurred, not because an innocent people has been slaughtered, not because circumcision has been weaponized — but because *his own position is now precarious*.

**לְהַבְאִישֵׁנִי בְּישֵׁב הָאָרֶץ** (*lehav'isheni beyoshev ha'arets*, "to make me stink among the inhabitants of the land") — the Hiphil infinitive of בָּאַשׁ (*ba'ash*, "to stink, to smell bad, to be offensive") — "to cause me to stink." This is a remarkably physical idiom for reputational damage: your deeds have made my name smell like rotting flesh to the people around us. Jacob's concern is entirely reputational and strategic: *ani metei mispar* ("I am few in number"), they will gather against me (*vene'esfu 'alay*), they will strike me (*vehikkuni*), I will be destroyed (*venishmadeti*). Every clause is first-person singular.

Jacob does not say: "You have done evil." He does not say: "You have violated the men of Shechem." He does not mention Dinah's welfare, or Hamor and Shechem's deaths, or the women and children now taken as plunder. His silence on the moral substance of what his sons have done — and his exclusive focus on his own vulnerability — is as much a textual datum as the massacre itself. Later, in Jacob's final testament (Genesis 49:5–7), he will curse Simeon and Levi's anger — but that is a different speech, in a different register.

EMET translates Jacob's speech word-for-word and offers no mitigation.

---

**34:31 — *hakezzonah* (הַכְזוֹנָה) — "As a Prostitute": The Sons' Final Word and the Chapter's Refusal to Resolve:**

The chapter's final verse is a single three-word question: **הַכְזוֹנָה יַעֲשֶׂה אֶת-אֲחֹתֵנוּ** (*hakezzonah ya'aseh et-achotenu*, "Shall he treat our sister as a prostitute?").

**הַ-** — the interrogative particle, opening a rhetorical question
**כְּ-** — the preposition "like, as"
**זוֹנָה** (*zonah*) — "prostitute, harlot, woman of promiscuous sexual availability"; the participial form of זָנָה (*zanah*, "to prostitute oneself, to fornicate"). The *zonah* is a woman without the protection of father, husband, or clan — sexually available without covenantal covering.

The question is devastating: Shechem took Dinah without negotiation, without bride-price, without her father's consent, and without any covenantal framework — the way one uses a *zonah*. The brothers are asserting: our sister is not a *zonah*. She is a daughter of Israel, a woman covered by covenant, and what was done to her was a fundamental violation of what she is.

The chapter ends here — no divine word, no resolution, no authorial verdict. Jacob has rebuked the brothers; the brothers have rebuked Jacob back. The narrative declines to adjudicate. The silence after this question is the chapter's most powerful statement.

The word *zonah* carries within this narrative an additional weight: the city of Shechem will later become associated with Israelite apostasy and "playing the harlot" (*zanah*) after other gods. Here, the word is used in its most literal sense — but the root *zanah* will haunt the Shechem traditions throughout Judges and beyond.

The LXX reads *hōs pornē chrēsetai tēi adelphēi hēmōn* (ὡς πόρνηι χρήσεται τῆι ἀδελφῇ ἡμῶν) — "shall he use our sister as a prostitute?" — *pornē* (πόρνη) being the standard Greek equivalent of *zonah*, and *chrēsetai* ("shall he use/treat") capturing the instrumental force exactly.

---

**LXX Vaticanus Observations (Pages 31–32):**

Codex Vaticanus pages 31–32 carry Genesis 34 in continuous Greek uncial script, without word-breaks or accents, in three columns per page. Page 31 bears the chapter annotation CAP. XXXVIII in the right margin; page 32 has Γεν XXXIX at the top center, indicating the LXX chapter break that opens with what corresponds to the beginning of the city-gate persuasion scene (vv. 18ff) in the MT.

Key LXX variants for Genesis 34:

1. **34:2 — *met' autēs*** — LXX uses *meta* ("with") for the second verb, softening the direct-object syntax of the MT which uses the accusative marker *et* (*vayyishkav otah*). As noted above, this may be a systematic Greek syntactic preference rather than a different Vorlage.

2. **34:3 — *parthenos*** — LXX renders MT *na'arah* ("young woman") as *parthenos* ("maiden, virgin") — an interpretive elevation that emphasizes Dinah's status as a virgin prior to the assault.

3. **34:7 — *anomia*** — LXX reads "lawlessness" (*anomia*, ἀνομία) for MT *nevalah* ("disgrace/outrage"). The choice of *anomia* frames the act in terms of Torah transgression rather than the MT's term of social-communal outrage.

4. **34:13 — *meta dolou*** — LXX "with guile" for MT *bemirmah* ("with deceit"). Exact equivalent.

5. **34:19 — "He was honored among all his father's house"** — LXX reads *endoxotatos* (ἐνδοξότατος, "most glorious/honored") for MT *nikbad* ("most honored"), preserving the superlative sense.

6. **34:21 — "Wide-handed" (*rachabat yadayim*)** — MT's idiomatic "wide of hands" (meaning "spacious, roomy, offering ample space for all") corresponds to LXX *plateia* (wide/broad) — a standard equivalent.

7. **34:25 — *en tōi ponōi*** — LXX "in the pain" for MT *ko'avim* ("in their being in pain"). Exact.

8. **34:30 — Jacob's complaint** — LXX *etaraxate me* (ἐταράξατέ με, "you have troubled/disturbed me") for MT *'akartem oti* ("you have brought trouble upon me"). *Tarassō* is the same verb used in the NT for the troubling of water (John 5:7) and the troubled heart (John 14:1). The word choice is slightly softer than the MT's *'akhar* ("stir up, roil, bring disaster").

9. **34:31 — *hōs pornē chrēsetai*** — LXX "shall he use [her] as a prostitute?" — exact rendering of the final devastating question.

**DSS Note:**
Per the chapter manifest, no significant Genesis 34 fragments from the Dead Sea Scrolls are extant. The Leningrad Codex (primary Hebrew witness, Folios 19v–20r) and Codex Vaticanus LXX (secondary Greek witness, Pages 31–32) are the two principal manuscript sources examined for this chapter.
